questions.question: 1. Which of the following sorting algorithms, in its typical implementation, gives the best performance when applied to an array that is sorted or almost sorted (with at most 1 or 2 elements misplaced)?

explanations.a: Incorrect. Quick Sort performs poorly on nearly sorted arrays due to its partitioning strategy, especially if a bad pivot is chosen.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Heap Sort does not take advantage of existing order in the array and always has O(n log n) complexity.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Merge Sort also does not benefit from existing order and always runs in O(n log n) time.
explanations.d: Correct. Insertion Sort is highly efficient for nearly sorted arrays, running in O(n) time in the best case.

questions.question: 2. Consider a binary min heap containing n elements, where every node has degree 2 (i.e., a full binary min heap tree). What is the probability of finding the largest element at the last level?

explanations.a: Incorrect. The largest element in a min heap is always at the last level, but not necessarily in half of the nodes.
explanations.b: Correct. In a full binary min heap, the largest element must be at the last level because all parent nodes are smaller.
explanations.c: Incorrect. The largest element is always at the last level, not just in 1 out of n cases.
explanations.d: Incorrect. 1/2^n is not related to the probability in this context.

questions.question: 3. In a max-heap, the element with the greatest key is always in which node?

explanations.a: Incorrect. The largest element in a max-heap is always at the root, not in a leaf node.
explanations.b: Incorrect. The first node of the left subtree does not necessarily contain the largest element.
explanations.c: Correct. By definition, the root node of a max-heap contains the greatest key.
explanations.d: Incorrect. The first node of the right subtree does not necessarily contain the largest element.

questions.question: 4. Heap can also be used as _______.

explanations.a: Correct. Heaps are commonly used to implement priority queues, where the highest (or lowest) priority element is always accessible.
explanations.b: Incorrect. A stack is a different data structure (LIFO), not implemented using a heap.
explanations.c: Incorrect. A heap does not maintain elements in strictly decreasing order.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Heaps are used as priority queues.

questions.question: 5. An array consists of n elements. We want to create a heap using these elements. The time complexity of building a heap will be in the order of ________.

explanations.a: Incorrect. O(n*n*log n) is much higher than the actual complexity for building a heap.
explanations.b: Correct. Building a heap from an array of n elements can be done in O(n) time using the bottom-up heap construction method.
explanations.c: Incorrect. O(n*n) is not the correct complexity for this operation.
explanations.d: Incorrect. O(n*log n*log n) is not the correct complexity for building a heap.
